EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across western Wisconsin. Eight tornadoes occurred across parts of Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. Four of these tornadoes were rated EF2 and three EF1. The most substantial damage from the EF2 tornadoes occurred in Clark County. One tornado destroyed a home in the southwest corner of the county near Five Mile Creek west of Neillsville (Clark County). Another tornado caused extensive damage to farms and trees after moving through Stanley (Chippewa County) into the northwest corner of Clark County. An EF0 tornado passed through Perrot State Park (Trempealeau County) and damaged several homes on the west edge of Trempealeau (Trempealeau County). The other tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings. Because of the severe weather threat, the Rotary Lights display in La Crosse (La Crosse County) was closed for the first time in over 26 years where two temporary structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A path of wind damage occurred across southwest Clark County from east of Humbird to near Globe. Most of the damage was uprooted or snapped trees but the roof was ripped off a metal pole shed and a wooded power pole was broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Upper Midwest from December 10th into the 11th. The storm brought snow and strong winds to most of western Wisconsin. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 11 inches. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred as gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th. The highest reported totals were 11.5 inches near Medford (Taylor County) and 10 inches near Owen (Clark County) and in Mondovi (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 8 to 11 inches of snow fell across Taylor County. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th when north winds gusted into the 30 to 40 mph range. The highest reported snowfall total was 11.5 inches north of Medford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong east to northeast winds of 20 to 30 knots with frequent gusts of 30 to 40 knots resulted in high waves of 8 to 12 feet for a 12 to 18 hour period from the evening of October 24th through the afternoon of October 25th.  A few gusts of 40 to 45 knots occurred as well.  The Lake Michigan water level was still about 3 feet above the long term average.  The combination of these factors produced high surf and localized lakeshore flooding along the Lake Michigan shore during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent high waves estimated at 8 to 12 feet lasted for about an 18 hour period and frequently overtopped a damaged break wall.  This resulted in damaging several moored vessels at the South Shore Yacht Club.  One vessel broke its mooring and washed up on a rocky shoreline area causing additional damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong east to northeast winds of 20 to 30 knots with frequent gusts of 30 to 40 knots resulted in high waves of 8 to 12 feet for a 12 to 18 hour period from the evening of October 24th through the afternoon of October 25th.  A few gusts of 40 to 45 knots occurred as well.  The Lake Michigan water level was still about 3 feet above the long term average.  The combination of these factors produced high surf and localized lakeshore flooding along the Lake Michigan shore during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent, strong onshore winds began the evening of October 24th and continued through the afternoon of October 25th.  These winds and above average Lake Michigan water levels caused lakeshore flooding at the McKinley Marina parking lot.  About a third of the parking lot was flooded.  Debris was also carried onshore by the persistent high waves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed north of a warm front then tracked east during the late afternoon hours and into the evening.  There was an isolated report of wind damage and several rounds of thunderstorms produced flash flooding during the overnight hours across portions of central Wisconsin as 3 to 5 inches of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dumped 3 to 5 inches of rain over northern Portage County during the evening hours. This caused several roads to be closed and barricaded due to flash flooding across much of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwestern Wisconsin continued to fare better in terms of drought conditions for the month of November. Only a limited area of D2 Severe Drought persisted from the Bayfield Peninsula into the Ashland area and far northern Iron County. Rainfall deficits of up to one-half inch were observed across the region with slightly above normal precipitation along the Bayfield Peninsula and across parts of Ashland, Iron and Price counties. While other parts of the area saw drought conditions worsen to D1 Moderate Drought, the area of D2 Severe Drought remained unchanged through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across Ashland County ranged from D0 Abnormally Dry over the far southern part of the county to D2 Severe Drought near Lake Superior to begin the month. Rainfall totals up to around a half inch above normal were observed  across the county through the month. D1 Moderate Drought spread across the remainder of the county with no change in the area of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 2nd. No additional changes in the drought status were observed through the remainder of the month despite the slightly above normal rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across Ashland County ranged from D0 Abnormally Dry over the southern part of the county to D2 Severe Drought near Chequamegon Bay to begin the month. Rainfall totals up to around a half inch above normal were observed in the far northeastern corner and much of the southeastern corner of the county with totals up to around a half inch below normal across the remainder of the county. D1 Moderate Drought spread across the remainder of the county with no change in the area of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 2nd. No additional changes in the drought status were observed through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavy snow showers developed along a stalled surface trough and brought whiteout conditions and slick roads over portions of I-41 in Washington County. Numerous multi-vehicle accidents were reported on I-41 near highway 144 and Cedar Creek Road, which included a crash involving 30 vehicles and another with 48 vehicles. 1 death was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavy snow showers produced whiteout conditions and slick roads over portions of I-41 in Washington County for a period of 45 minutes to 90 minutes. Numerous multi-vehicle accidents were reported on I-41 near highway 144 and Cedar Creek Road, which included a crash involving 30 vehicles and another with 48 vehicles. 1 death was reported. 12 people were injured and sent to the hospital. Another 26 people were treated at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the western Great Lakes brought a wintry mix and then heavy snow. Snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were recorded across much of the region. Snowfall totals across east-central Wisconsin near Lake Michigan were lower due to a longer period of mixed precipitation. ||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 12.0 inches near Phelps (Vilas Co.) and Lac Vieux Desert (Vilas Co.), 11.6 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.), 11.1 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), and 10.9 inches near Boulder Junction (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix brought around a tenth of an inch of icing before changing over to heavy snow. Gusty northwest winds also caused some blowing and drifting of the new snow late Thursday evening into Friday morning. The highest snowfall totals across Portage County included 8.0 inches in Rosholt, 7.9 inches in Stevens Point, Rosholt 8.0 inches, 7.2 inches in Amherst, and 6.5 inches in Plover and Almond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the Northland lead to the development of widespread snowfall across the region. This system was originally expected to move through quickly but slowed down as it moved into northwest Wisconsin. Heavy banded snow began to develop and it quickly became evident that more snow than expected was going to occur. By the end of the event, snowfall totals of 5 to 8 inches were reported with a high report of 8.6 inches near Mercer, Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing wave of low pressure brought breezy southwest winds to far southern WI. A cluster of trees fell on a garage in Delavan. Afternoon wind gusts measured around 45 mph over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb fell onto a car in Waukesha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado Low exited the eastern slope of the Rockies and moved towards the Great Lakes producing snow across the region. Several key ingredients were expected to come together with this system including favorable moisture within the snow growth zone and strong signals for frontogenesis leading to enhanced lift. Models struggled with the track of this system and, while snow was expected across the region, the heaviest snow ended up setting up across northwest Wisconsin as opposed to eastern Wisconsin. Very heavy snowfall rates were observed with a location in Burnett County receiving 7 inches of accumulation in about 3 hours. Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were received with a high of 9.8 inches near Manitowish, Wisconsin.

The last night of the arctic outbreak was once again widespread, affecting almost all of the Northland. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado Low exited the eastern slope of the Rockies and moved towards the Great Lakes producing snow across the region. Several key ingredients were expected to come together with this system including favorable moisture within the snow growth zone and strong signals for frontogenesis leading to enhanced lift. Models struggled with the track of this system and, while snow was expected across the region, the heaviest snow ended up setting up across northwest Wisconsin as opposed to eastern Wisconsin. Very heavy snowfall rates were observed with a location in Burnett County receiving 7 inches of accumulation in about 3 hours. Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were received with a high of 9.8 inches near Manitowish, Wisconsin.

The last two nights of the arctic outbreak were the most widespread in terms extreme wind chills. On the night of the 13th, a surface low over Lake Superior coupled with high pressure over the Northern Plains set up a gradient over the Northland, keeping winds in place through the night and into the morning of the 14th. The entirety of the Northland saw extreme wind chills and air temperatures in the 30s and 40s below zero. This was the eighth night of an arctic outbreak across the region that lasted over a week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across the region on February 4th. This storm dropped between 4 and 9 inches of snow that caused roads to become snow covered and slippery. Numerous schools across the area canceled classes for the day. A COVID-19 testing site in Monroe County was canceled because of the snow. The highest reported totals were 9 inches in Lynxville (Crawford County) and 8.7 inches near Friendship (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 8 inches of snow fell across Adams County. The highest reported total was 8.7 inches near Friendship.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shot of arctic air settled in over north central Wisconsin and produced dangerously cold wind chills of 35 to 45 below zero from the early morning of February 14th into the morning of the 15th. The wind chills occurred with air temperatures in the single digits and teens below zero along with winds of 10 to 15 mph. The coldest recorded wind chill was -42 on the morning of the 14th by the weather observing equipment at the Medford airport (Taylor County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills of 35 to 45 below zero occurred from the early morning of February 14th into the morning of the 15th. These wind chills occurred with temperatures in the single digits and teens below zero and winds of 10 to 15 mph. The coldest recorded wind chill was 42 below zero by the automated weather observing equipment at the Medford airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold air with temperatures from 10 to 20 below zero settled in over north central Wisconsin during the early morning hours of February 7th. Combined with northwest winds around 10 mph, dangerously cold winds chills of 40 to 45 below zero occurred. The coldest recorded wind chill was -45 by the observing equipment at the Medford airport (Taylor County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of air temperatures in the teens below zero and northwest winds around 10 mph produced dangerously cold wind chills between 40 and 45 below zero. The coldest recorded wind chill was 45 below zero by the automated weather observing equipment at the Medford airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A western Wisconsin man died as a result of hypothermia in early February. The Town of Genoa (Vernon County) man did not have any heat in the house and likely passed away sometime on February 7th when air temperatures were in the 10 to 20 below zero and wind chills were 25 to 30 below zero. The body was discovered by authorities on February 10th, but the exact date of the death is unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Town of Genoa man was discovered dead in his residence by authorities on February 10th. The man's house did not have any heat and it is estimated that he passed away sometime on February 7th when temperatures were in the teens below zero and wind chills were 20 to 30 below zero. The official cause of death was listed as exposure/hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the western Great Lakes brought a wintry mix and then heavy snow. Snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were recorded across much of the region. Snowfall totals across east-central Wisconsin near Lake Michigan were lower due to a longer period of mixed precipitation. ||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 12.0 inches near Phelps (Vilas Co.) and Lac Vieux Desert (Vilas Co.), 11.6 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.), 11.1 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), and 10.9 inches near Boulder Junction (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix brought around a tenth of an inch of icing before changing over to heavy snow. Gusty northwest winds also caused some blowing and drifting of the new snow late Thursday evening into Friday morning. The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 12.0 inches near Phelps and Lac Vieux Desert, 11.6 inches in Eagle River, 11.0 inches south of Eagle River, and 10.9 inches near Boulder Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a warm front lifting north during the evening hours.  The strongest storms produced marginally severe hail across central Wisconsin. Lightning struck a house in Fremont, setting on fire and burning it to the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on Highway 110 in Fremont during the late evening hours, causing it to burn to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty southerly winds estimated from 40 to 50 mph, due to a wake low pressure area, caused the top half of a 30 foot Oak Tree to snap off in far southeast Fond du Lac County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The top half of a 30 foot oak tree snapped off in the early morning hours at a location just southeast of Campbellsport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent below normal precipitation since February 2021 allowed severe drought conditions to develop across parts of western Wisconsin in late June, including La Crosse, Monroe, Richland and Grant Counties. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches during the last part of June allowed soil moisture conditions to improve and brought an end to the severe drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent below normal precipitation since February 2021 allowed severe drought conditions to develop across parts of Grant County. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches during the last part of June allowed soil moisture conditions to improve and brought an end to the severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A young teenage swimmer at North Beach along the Racine lakefront was found unconscious about 200 yards from shore.  The swimmer was flown to a local hospital but succumbed to injuries sustained from drowning two days later.  Onshore winds and waves may have caused long shore and structural currents along North Beach and the North Beach Pier which may have contributed to the drowning death.

A young woman succumbed to drowning injuries shortly after being pulled from the water at Zoo Beach along the Racine Wisconsin lakefront.  Northeast onshore winds of 10 to 15 mph caused waves of 1 to 3 feet.  The onshore winds and waves may have caused longshore and structural currents along the beach and North Pier which contributed to the young woman drowning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Young woman succumbed to drowning injuries which occurred at Zoo Beach along the Racine lakefront, shortly after being pulled from the water.  Onshore winds and waves may have caused longshore and structural currents along the beach and North Pier which may have contributed to the drowning. According to witnesses, the young woman was swimming in a shallow area and a underwater current carried her to deep water.  Officials stated the young woman did not know how to swim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, high waves and cold water on Lake Winnebago, located just north of the city of Fond du Lac, contributed to a canoe capsizing, leading to two direct deaths.  Neither victim was wearing a personal flotation device.  Winds were gusting as high as 45 to 50 mph near the time of the event, and waves on Lake Winnebago were estimated to be 4 to 5 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and high waves on Lake Winnebago resulted in a canoe capsizing during the evening of March 29th.  The canoe was located about one mile north of the city of Fond du Lac and the Fond du Lac river outlet, on Lake Winnebago.  Two young male canoeists, neither wearing a personal flotation device, fell into the cold lake water and drowned.  The Lake Winnebago water temperature was estimated to be 42 degrees F. Winds were gusting as high as 40 knots near the time of the event, and waves on the southern end of Lake Winnebago were estimated to be 4 to 5 feet.  The victims were located several days later on March 31st and April 1st.  Strong and gusty south winds had been occurring over the lake and vicinity since late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Monday, April 5th, a frontal boundary was lying across southwest Minnesota, then northeast toward northwest Wisconsin. Ahead of this front, temperatures rose into the low to mid-80s with modest MLCAPEs of 1000 J/Kg. Mid-level lapse rates were quite impressive and over 8 C/Km. Thus, the potential of large hail was evident as effective wind shear values were 40-50 knots. By the afternoon, several thunderstorms developed in Minnesota and moved northeast into west-central Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire in the Necedah Wildlife Refuge (Juneau County) burned 257 acres on April 4th. The fire was started in the late morning by a passing train. Most of the land that was burned was classified by the Wisconsin DNR as non-timbered marsh, bog or lowland brush. Weather conditions from the nearby Necedah RAWS site indicated the relative humidity during the fire ranged from 20 to 25 percent and south winds gusted between 20 and 26 mph with temperatures in the 70s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire burned 257 acres in the Necedah Wildlife Refuge on April 4th. The Wisconsin DNR classified the burned ground cover as non-timbered marsh, bog or lowland brush. The fire was started in the late morning by a passing train locomotive. Weather conditions during the fire from a nearby RAWS station indicated the relative humidity ranged from 20 to 25 percent, south winds gusted from 20 to 26 mph with temperatures in the 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure area in the upper atmosphere brought a brief period of moderate to heavy snow to portions of southern WI. Areas of light freezing drizzle and sleet followed the snow. The falling snow and easterly wind gusts of 25-30 mph made for very low visibility at times to 1/4 mile or less during the rush hour. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1-3 inches followed by areas of freezing drizzle and sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Upper Midwest from December 10th into the 11th. The storm brought snow and strong winds to most of western Wisconsin. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 11 inches. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred as gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th. The highest reported totals were 11.5 inches near Medford (Taylor County) and 10 inches near Owen (Clark County) and in Mondovi (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across Adams County. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th when north winds gusted into the 30 to 40 mph range. The highest reported snowfall total was 7 inches east of New Rome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east across the Ohio River Valley, bringing widespread light to moderate snow to southern Wisconsin. The track of this system allowed lake enhanced snow to develop under east to northeast flow for the counties bordering Lake Michigan, which is where the heaviest snow fell during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of 3-5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east across the Ohio River Valley, bringing widespread light to moderate snow to southern Wisconsin. The track of this system allowed lake enhanced snow to develop under east to northeast flow for the counties bordering Lake Michigan, which is where the heaviest snow fell during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east across the Ohio River Valley, bringing widespread light to moderate snow to southern Wisconsin. The track of this system allowed lake enhanced snow to develop under east to northeast flow for the counties bordering Lake Michigan, which is where the heaviest snow fell during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of 6 to 8 inches of snow across the western half of Racine County. The eastern half of the county saw higher snowfall totals, with reports ranging from 10 to 13 inches of snow. East to northeast winds gusting to 30-35 mph caused significant blowing and drifting snow, and contributed to reduced visibilities during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well organized area of low pressure moved through the Ohio River Valley into the Lower Great Lakes. The low spread a prolonged swath of moderate, powdery snow across southern WI. Lake enhanced snow added to the overall snow totals over far eastern WI. Brisk winds led to blowing and drifting snow and limited visibility. Numerous school closures occurred across the area. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred especially in metro areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 5-9 inches. Brisk winds and blowing and drifting snow. A COVID-19 test site was closed due to the winter storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Midwest from January 25th into the 26th. This storm produced a band of heavy snow from southeast Nebraska across Iowa into southern Wisconsin. The heavy snow band produced accumulations of 6 to 9 inches across Grant County where the highest reported snow total was 9 inches near Burton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 6 to 9 inches of snow across Grant County. The highest reported total was 9 inches near Burton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked through northern Wisconsin during the early afternoon hours of February 28.  Mainly snow fell across far northern Wisconsin with a mix of snow, rain, and freezing drizzle across central and northeast Wisconsin. A heavy band of 6 to 9 inches of snow fell across Vilas County, with 4 to 5 inches across northwest Oneida County in a few short hours.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 8.8 inches 9 miles east northeast of Boulder Junction (Vilas Co.), 7.3 inches near Boulder Junction (Vilas Co.), 6 inches near Phelps and Eagle River (Vilas Co.), and 5 inches near Willow Reservoir (Oneida Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 8.8 inches 9 miles east northeast of Boulder Junction, 7.3 inches near Boulder Junction, and 6 inches near Eagle River and Phelps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold air plunged from Canada into the Upper Mississippi River Valley and western Great Lakes bringing wind chill temperatures of 20 below to 35 below zero. Warm shelters were opened in urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures mainly from 25 below to 34 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed when they lost control of the vehicle they were driving on snow covered roads. The accident happened on Interstate 90 near Tomah (Monroe County) on the morning of February 12th. The driver of the vehicle lost control with the vehicle entering the median, flipping and coming to rest in the opposite lanes of traffic. The driver was ejected and hit by a car. Snowfall reports indicate only light accumulations of a half to one inch occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in an accident on Interstate 90 near Tomah. The driver lost control on snow covered roads with the vehicle going into the median, flipping and coming to rest in the opposite lanes of traffic. The driver was ejected and struck by another car. COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between a half and one inch of snow fell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Oneida County included 5 inches near Willow Reservoir and 4.8 inches near Hazelhurst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first week of February, an Arctic air mass moved southward across the Upper Midwest and continued through the middle of the month. The core of the cold air remained centered across south-central Canada before slowly weakening and moving off to the east after the weekend of February 13th and 14th. Several days of well below normal temperatures combined with subtle wind speeds, generated periods of wind chill values below -30F around Rice Lake and Ladysmith. The bulk of the lowest wind chills, when combined with temperatures dropping to around -20F to -25F, were Sunday morning, February 14th. Due to the terrain, and more sheltering from the higher winds speeds in the valleys, wind chill values of -35F or lower occurred a few hours either side of sunrise. The lowest wind chill occurred at Cumberland airport where a reading of -46 was recorded at 0735 LST. ||A secondary surge of low wind chill values occurred late Sunday and into Monday morning February 15th, especially across west-central Wisconsin where wind speeds were slightly higher based on the tighter pressure gradient. Actual temperatures were much lower and winds were lighter. However, even a slight 5 to 8 mph wind speed caused wind chill values to drop below -35F for several hours. ||Wind chill values were obtained from ASOS, AWOS, RAWS, and other private weather wind sensors throughout west-central Wisconsin.||The following are some of the lowest wind chill values during the height of the cold snap:||-47F at Cumberland, Wisconsin. |-44F at Woodville, Wisconsin. |-43F at Menomonie, Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather sensors provided wind chill values during this prolonged cold snap. The lowest wind chill value of -46F occurred near Cumberland at 0730 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved across portions of western Wisconsin during the afternoon of May 25th as a cold front moved across the region. These storms produced damaging winds across parts of Buffalo, Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. Winds estimated to be around 60 mph blew down trees and flipped semi trailers and a mobile home in the vicinity of Arcadia (Trempealeau County). Trees were also blown down near Merrillan (Clark County) and across the northwest part of Jackson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown in unspecified locations across Trempealeau County. Some of these trees went down on homes and power lines. One mobile home was flipped by the winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down east of Humbird.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of warm air advection ahead of an approaching cold front brought widespread precipitation to southern Wisconsin. Precipitation started as rain and freezing rain in spots, but transitioned over to snow during the afternoon and evening hours. Many schools either cancelled ahead of time or had early dismissals early that afternoon. Snow accumulated quickly, impacting the evening commute. A strong cold front arrived that evening, bringing strong wind gusts of 30-40 mph. These winds caused blowing and drifting snow across much of southern Wisconsin as snow was winding down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started off as freezing rain, but transitioned over to moderate snow. Highest snowfall totals came in at 5.5 inches in Portage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started off as freezing rain, which caused a light glaze of ice on untreated surfaces and slick spots across Dane County. Precipitation transitioned over to moderate snow, with 3 to 5 inches of snow falling across the county.

A strong cold front arrived that evening, bringing strong wind gusts of 30-40 mph. These winds caused blowing and drifting snow across much of southern Wisconsin as snow was winding down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow, with around 2 inches of snow reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe thunderstorm that produced wind damage across eastern Goodhue County in Minnesota, moved east-northeast across west central Wisconsin during the early afternoon. A wind gust of 75 mph occurred at the Red Wing Airport in Pierce County. As the storm continued to move through Pierce, Dunn and Chippewa Counties of west central Wisconsin, several reports of down trees and power lines occurred.

An upper level disturbance brought light, powdery snow to southern WI during very cold conditions. The small amount of snow created very slippery road conditions, resulting in numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents on Interstates and in urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation 1-2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first week of February, an Arctic air mass moved southward across the Upper Midwest and continued through the middle of the month. The core of the cold air remained centered across south-central Canada before slowly weakening and moving off to the east after the weekend of February 13th and 14th. Several days of well below normal temperatures combined with subtle wind speeds, generated periods of wind chill values below -30F around Rice Lake and Ladysmith. The bulk of the lowest wind chills, when combined with temperatures dropping to around -20F to -25F, were Sunday morning, February 14th. Due to the terrain, and more sheltering from the higher winds speeds in the valleys, wind chill values of -35F or lower occurred a few hours either side of sunrise. The lowest wind chill occurred at Cumberland airport where a reading of -46 was recorded at 0735 LST. ||A secondary surge of low wind chill values occurred late Sunday and into Monday morning February 15th, especially across west-central Wisconsin where wind speeds were slightly higher based on the tighter pressure gradient. Actual temperatures were much lower and winds were lighter. However, even a slight 5 to 8 mph wind speed caused wind chill values to drop below -35F for several hours. ||Wind chill values were obtained from ASOS, AWOS, RAWS, and other private weather wind sensors throughout west-central Wisconsin.||The following are some of the lowest wind chill values during the height of the cold snap:||-47F at Cumberland, Wisconsin. |-44F at Woodville, Wisconsin. |-43F at Menomonie, Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather sensors provided wind chill values during this prolonged cold snap. The lowest wind chill value of -47F occurred near Cumberland at 0500 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lifting north across eastern Kentucky and Ohio brought wrap around snow to southeast and east-central Wisconsin on the evening of February 15. Northeast flow developing over Lake Michigan and favorable unstable conditions over the lake led to a period of lake-enhanced snow along the Lake Michigan shoreline. As fetch remained favorable over Lake Michigan and cold air advection increased, a single dominant lake-effect snow band become well organized during the early morning hours on February 16. This lake-effect snow band remained anchored over areas near Milwaukee and south to the Wisconsin/Illinois border, keeping the focus for high snowfall totals along the lakeshore. On the afternoon hours on February 16, the lake-effect snow band finally pushed offshore over the open waters of Lake Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sharp gradient of heavy snow developed along the I-94/41, with 2 to 6 inches to the west of Kenosha. Higher snowfall totals of 8 to 10 inches of snow were along the I-94/41 corridor. Closer the the Lake Michigan shoreline, snowfall totals of 12 to 16 inches of snow were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across western Wisconsin. Eight tornadoes occurred across parts of Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. Four of these tornadoes were rated EF2 and three EF1. The most substantial damage from the EF2 tornadoes occurred in Clark County. One tornado destroyed a home in the southwest corner of the county near Five Mile Creek west of Neillsville (Clark County). Another tornado caused extensive damage to farms and trees after moving through Stanley (Chippewa County) into the northwest corner of Clark County. An EF0 tornado passed through Perrot State Park (Trempealeau County) and damaged several homes on the west edge of Trempealeau (Trempealeau County). The other tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings. Because of the severe weather threat, the Rotary Lights display in La Crosse (La Crosse County) was closed for the first time in over 26 years where two temporary structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the Platteville airport measured a 68 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across western Wisconsin. Eight tornadoes occurred across parts of Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. Four of these tornadoes were rated EF2 and three EF1. The most substantial damage from the EF2 tornadoes occurred in Clark County. One tornado destroyed a home in the southwest corner of the county near Five Mile Creek west of Neillsville (Clark County). Another tornado caused extensive damage to farms and trees after moving through Stanley (Chippewa County) into the northwest corner of Clark County. An EF0 tornado passed through Perrot State Park (Trempealeau County) and damaged several homes on the west edge of Trempealeau (Trempealeau County). The other tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings. Because of the severe weather threat, the Rotary Lights display in La Crosse (La Crosse County) was closed for the first time in over 26 years where two temporary structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches were blown down east of Gotham.

Eight tornadoes occurred across parts of Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. Four of these tornadoes were rated EF2 and three EF1. The most substantial damage from the EF2 tornadoes occurred in Clark County. One tornado destroyed a home in the southwest corner of the county near Five Mile Creek west of Neillsville (Clark County). Another tornado caused extensive damage to farms and trees after moving through Stanley (Chippewa County) into the northwest corner of Clark County. An EF0 tornado passed through Perrot State Park (Trempealeau County) and damaged several homes on the west edge of Trempealeau (Trempealeau County). The other tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings. Because of the severe weather threat, the Rotary Lights display in La Crosse (La Crosse County) was closed for the first time in over 26 years where two temporary structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF2 tornado developed in Jackson County and moved into the southwest part of Clark County east of Humbird. The tornado continued to move northeast before dissipating along County Highway B west of Sidney. This part of Clark County is heavily forested and thousands of trees were damaged.

A young woman pulled from the waters of Lake Michigan in Racine WI died about two weeks later on July 1st.  Emergency crews responded to North Beach in Racine late in the afternoon on June 21st, to two teenage girls who were experiencing an emergency in Lake Michigan.  The first girl was rescued with cuts and scratches on her body.  The second girl received medical attention at the scene and was airlifted to a local hospital, where she later succumbed to her injuries.  Emergency officials on the scene noted a strong current appeared to pull the young woman into deeper waters and the rocks next to a break wall at the south end of North Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Young woman pulled from Lake Michigan waters at North Beach in Racine WI later succumbed to her drowning injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe thunderstorm that produced wind damage across eastern Goodhue County in Minnesota, moved east-northeast across west central Wisconsin during the early afternoon. A wind gust of 75 mph occurred at the Red Wing Airport in Pierce County. As the storm continued to move through Pierce, Dunn and Chippewa Counties of west central Wisconsin, several reports of down trees and power lines occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees and power lines were blown down near and south of Menomonie. One of the trees damaged an apartment building near a high school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over west-central Wisconsin during the afternoon of Thursday, June 24th. These storms developed in an area of strong instability and ample wind shear to cause rotating storms. A tornado formed near Bruce, Wisconsin and caused damage to XX. As the storm moved southeast, and built to the west, large hail fell near Bloomer, Wisconsin and parts of Chippewa and Eau Claire counties before moving off to the southeast. Another brief tornado occurred on the south side of Stanley and was caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved toward the north-northwest and struck a farm toward the end of its path. The roof of a shed was lifted up and pushed, some concrete blocks were knocked over. Siding of a house was ripped off. A few trees were downed. The starting point was based on video. Three videos were available. The damage survey of the farm was provided by a storm chaser.

Lightning struck a hour in Kiel, causing a fire, as well as a pole in Green Bay, which damaged 2 garages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over west-central Wisconsin during the afternoon of Thursday, June 24th. These storms developed in an area of strong instability and ample wind shear to cause rotating storms. A tornado formed near Bruce, Wisconsin and caused damage to XX. As the storm moved southeast, and built to the west, large hail fell near Bloomer, Wisconsin and parts of Chippewa and Eau Claire counties before moving off to the southeast. Another brief tornado occurred on the south side of Stanley and was caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video of a tornado was provided via a TV station's social media site. The tornado was over a field and caused no known damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across western Wisconsin. Eight tornadoes occurred across parts of Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. Four of these tornadoes were rated EF2 and three EF1. The most substantial damage from the EF2 tornadoes occurred in Clark County. One tornado destroyed a home in the southwest corner of the county near Five Mile Creek west of Neillsville (Clark County). Another tornado caused extensive damage to farms and trees after moving through Stanley (Chippewa County) into the northwest corner of Clark County. An EF0 tornado passed through Perrot State Park (Trempealeau County) and damaged several homes on the west edge of Trempealeau (Trempealeau County). The other tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings. Because of the severe weather threat, the Rotary Lights display in La Crosse (La Crosse County) was closed for the first time in over 26 years where two temporary structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF1 tornado developed southeast of Osseo and moved northeast, crossing into Jackson County near County Highway G. The tornado passed through a farm where damage occurred to the house and outbuildings. Otherwise, mainly tree damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwestern Wisconsin continued to fare better in terms of drought conditions for the month of October. Only a limited area of D2 Severe Drought persisted from the Bayfield Peninsula into the Ashland area and far northern Iron County. Rainfall deficits of 1 to 2 inches were observed across the region and while other parts of the area saw drought conditions worsen to D0 Abnormally Dry or D1 Moderate Drought, the area of D2 Severe Drought remained unchanged through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across Iron County ranged from None in the far southern portion of the county to D2 Severe Drought adjacent to Lake Superior to begin the month. Rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were observed over the month which is about an inch or so below normal. While the area of D2 Severe Drought did not change significantly through the month, the southern part of the county did worsen to D0 Abnormally Dry with the update on the 5th. No further changes were observed across the county with the subsequent updates for October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved across western Wisconsin during the morning of July 27th. These storms produced damaging winds and large hail.  Behind the first line of storms, redevelopment occurred during the late morning with these storms also producing some large hail. The largest reported hail with the second storms was tennis ball sized east of Galesville (Trempealeau County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall remained below normal for most of northwestern Wisconsin through the month of August keeping much of the region in the D0 Abnormally Dry category for the month. Some areas of D1 Moderate Drought and D2 Severe Drought spread across the region as the dry pattern persisted, but a rain event towards the end of the month led to some improvement and the removal of D2 Severe Drought in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of D0 Abnormally Dry and D1 Moderate Drought were seen across Burnett County through the month. As the dry pattern persisted, the far southwestern corner of the county entered into D2 Severe Drought on August 17th. A moderate rainfall event worked across the area at the end of the month and led to the removal of D2 Moderate Drought with the August 31st update leaving D0 and D1 conditions across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifted a warm front into the Northland during the day on the 26th. Behind this warm front was a moist and unstable airmass and daytime heating led to the development of strong to severe storms over northern Minnesota that moved to the southeast during the evening hours. While Northwest Wisconsin didn't see much from the first round of storms, later storms overnight produced hail up to 3 in diameter and damage to trees as they moved across the region. The KDLH radar was inoperable for this event but a high level of service was maintained by utilizing the KMPX, KMQT, KGRB and KARX radars and one minute GOES-16 satellite data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northeast Wisconsin during the evening hours ahead of an approaching cold front.  The strongest storms produced a swath of damaging winds, with wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph, and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed at 8:40 PM CDT near Pine River in southern Lincoln County and produced damage to dozens of trees, (DI 28, DOD 2,3,4), continuing south and entering Marathon County at 8:42 PM CDT. Total path length is estimated to be 2.68 miles long, with a width of 300 yards. Peak wind estimated to be 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of Tuesday, July 6th. This storm intensified as it moved across the Mississippi River and northeast toward Eau Claire County of west central Wisconsin. This storm bowed out as it moved into southern Eau Claire County where trees were blown down near the Trempealeau and Eau Claire border. Other trees were blown down as it moved across southern Eau Claire County before moving off to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 18 inch diameter tree was blown down southwest of Allen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of an outflow boundary during the evening hours across east-central Wisconsin. The strongest storms produced damaging winds and a tornado. Rainfall amounts around 2 inches fell in Green Bay, producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed at 5:58 pm CDT northwest of Black Creek in Outagamie County and continued east southeast before dissipating at 6:11 pm CDT.  The tornado produced damage to trees (DI 28, DOD 2,3,4) and to the siding and roof to a house (DI 2, DOD 2). Total path length is estimated to be 2.05 miles long, with a width of 40 yards. Peak wind estimated to be around 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early evening of Wednesday, July 28th, scattered thunderstorms developed along a slowly moving frontal boundary across Northern Wisconsin. A very warm layer above the surface kept most of the activity isolated until early evening as thunderstorms developed north of St. Croix Falls to Ladysmith, Wisconsin. These storms continued to build southward, and a few developed over east-central Minnesota near Chisago City before moving into Polk County, Wisconsin. ||During the evening, the storms congealed into a solid line of severe storms as they tracked southward across west central Wisconsin. ||As the storms moved southward, several tight circulations developed, one stronger circulation near New Richmond produced a tornado southeast of town. This tornado damaged about one dozen homes, mostly due to shingles, soffits or siding being torn off. One house, however, had windows blown in and had partial roof damage. It also had a garage that was completely blown apart and destroyed, but the garage had been added on and was not part of the original house itself. Another house had damage to its front porch overhang, which allowed wind to enter the attic and punch a hole through the roof on the backside of the house. At a farm, an old barn collapsed. At another house, part of the roof was blown off a machine shed.||Other storm surveys revealed two areas of significant downburst winds. One was determined that a downburst blew down hundreds of trees along a broad swath from 4 miles SSW of Deer Park, to 9 miles north of Baldwin. The trees were blown down from the north, northeast or east. There was roof damage to one local business. The second area was determined that a downburst hit the west side of Spring Valley. Numerous trees, sheds and power lines were blown down. A school gymnasium roof was damaged and a lot of the debris landed on houses and vehicles. A local resident stated that the severe winds persisted for several minutes.||Outside of these damaged areas, there was minor damage to trees and power lines over Barron, Dunn, Rusk, Chippewa, St. Croix and Pierce counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About one dozen homes were damaged, mostly due to some shingles, soffits or siding being torn off.  One house, however, had windows blown in and had partial roof damage.  It also had a garage that was completely blown apart and destroyed, but the garage had been added on and was not part of the original house itself. Another house had damage to its front porch overhang, which allowed wind to enter the attic and punch a hole through the roof on the backside of the house. At a farm, an old barn collapsed. At another house, part of the roof was blown off a machine shed.

Another house had damage to its front porch overhang, which allowed wind to enter the attic and punch a hole through the roof on the backside of the house. At a farm, an old barn collapsed. At another house, part of the roof was blown off a machine shed.||Other storm surveys revealed two areas of significant downburst winds. One was determined that a downburst blew down hundreds of trees along a broad swath from 4 miles SSW of Deer Park, to 9 miles north of Baldwin. The trees were blown down from the north, northeast or east. There was roof damage to one local business. The second area was determined that a downburst hit the west side of Spring Valley. Numerous trees, sheds and power lines were blown down. A school gymnasium roof was damaged and a lot of the debris landed on houses and vehicles. A local resident stated that the severe winds persisted for several minutes.||Outside of these damaged areas, there was minor damage to trees and power lines over Barron, Dunn, Rusk, Chippewa, St. Croix and Pierce counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down in Fairchild.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifted a warm front into the Northland during the day on the 26th. Behind this warm front was a moist and unstable airmass and daytime heating led to the development of strong to severe storms over northern Minnesota that moved to the southeast during the evening hours. While Northwest Wisconsin didn't see much from the first round of storms, later storms overnight produced hail up to 3 in diameter and damage to trees as they moved across the region. The KDLH radar was inoperable for this event but a high level of service was maintained by utilizing the KMPX, KMQT, KGRB and KARX radars and one minute GOES-16 satellite data.

A couple rounds of storms moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon of July 6th. Wind gusts blew down trees in parts of Buffalo, Trempealeau, La Crosse and Clark Counties. The most extensive damage occurred southwest of Eleva (Trempealeau County) where hundreds of trees were downed in two small forested areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Modena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms across portions of east-central and southeast Wisconsin brought multiple rounds of heavy rain, resulting in flash flooding across portions of Ozaukee and Milwaukee counties late August 7th into the early morning hours on August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts quickly totaled 3 to 5 inches over northern and western Milwaukee County after repeated rounds of heavy rain. Reports of water over Interstate 41 near Wauwatosa, and water completely covering the roads along the Root River Parkway near Cleveland and Oklahoma Streets in West Allis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged dry spell across much of northwestern Wisconsin continued into July with rainfall deficits of 3 to 6 inches observed for the year through the middle of the month. Drought conditions were slow to spread across the area with only a small portion of Burnett County being place into Severe Drought during the month. Little relief is expected as a warm dry pattern looks to remain in place into August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall deficits of 3 to 6 inches were observed across Burnett County by the middle of July. A small area of D2 Severe Drought entered the far southwestern corner of the county on July 13 and persisted through the end of the month as the warm and dry pattern persisted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northeast Wisconsin during the evening hours ahead of an approaching cold front.  The strongest storms produced a swath of damaging winds, with wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph, and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that formed at 8:40 PM CDT in southern Lincoln County crossed the county line at 8:42 PM CDT.  The tornado continued south and produced damage to dozens of trees, (DI 28, DOD 2,3,4) before ending near the intersection of Shady Lane Road and Hazelwood Lane at 8:44 PM CDT. Total path length is estimated to be 2.68 miles long, with a width of 300 yards. Peak wind estimated to be 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across western Wisconsin. Eight tornadoes occurred across parts of Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. Four of these tornadoes were rated EF2 and three EF1. The most substantial damage from the EF2 tornadoes occurred in Clark County. One tornado destroyed a home in the southwest corner of the county near Five Mile Creek west of Neillsville (Clark County). Another tornado caused extensive damage to farms and trees after moving through Stanley (Chippewa County) into the northwest corner of Clark County. An EF0 tornado passed through Perrot State Park (Trempealeau County) and damaged several homes on the west edge of Trempealeau (Trempealeau County). The other tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings. Because of the severe weather threat, the Rotary Lights display in La Crosse (La Crosse County) was closed for the first time in over 26 years where two temporary structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF1 tornado formed west of Greenwood near the intersection of County Highways G and O. The tornado was only on the ground for about a mile but caused extensive damage to two farms. A barn roof was removed, a pole shed destroyed, power pole snapped and roof damage to an outbuilding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was 1 of 17 tornadoes to occur in Wisconsin on the night of July 28th into the early morning hours of July 29th. This was the 13th overall tornado for the event. A line of storms developed in northern Wisconsin and pushed south into southern Wisconsin in the overnight hours of the 28th into the 29th of July with mainly brief tornadoes and damaging winds occurring. ||This tornado began a few country blocks east of tornado #12 and started near County Highway P near Aliceton Dr and then tracked to the south, damaging a number of structures along County Rd E, crossing I-94 before going through Concord.  The tornado then went through rural areas before ending east of Willow Glen Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of storms moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon of August 7th. One of these storms produced an EF3 tornado that started in Boscobel (Grant County) and traveled east/southeast before dissipating south of Blue River (Grant County). Along the path of the tornado, 6 homes sustained enough damage to be considered total losses but no injuries occurred. Flash flooding caused a mud slide along State Highway 33 southeast of La Crosse (La Crosse County) and washed out part of County Road NN south of St. Joseph (La Crosse County). Water also covered portions of State Highway 27 and County Roads J and X near Melvina (Monroe County). Coon Creek went out of its banks and caused extensive damage to the baseball field in Coon Valley (Vernon County). A county wide disaster was declared by Vernon County officials because of the flood damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey confirmed that an EF3 tornado occurred across northern Grant County. The tornado started in Boscobel where damage to trees, power poles and power lines occurred. The tornado then moved east/southeast before dissipating south of Blue River. Along the path, six homes sustained enough damage to be considered total losses while numerous other had minor to moderate damage. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Upper Midwest from December 10th into the 11th. The storm brought snow and strong winds to most of western Wisconsin. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 11 inches. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred as gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th. The highest reported totals were 11.5 inches near Medford (Taylor County) and 10 inches near Owen (Clark County) and in Mondovi (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across La Crosse County. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th when north winds gusted into the 30 to 40 mph range. The highest reported snowfall total was 7.1 inches just north of Holmen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Upper Midwest from December 10th into the 11th. The storm brought snow and strong winds to most of western Wisconsin. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 11 inches. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred as gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th. The highest reported totals were 11.5 inches near Medford (Taylor County) and 10 inches near Owen (Clark County) and in Mondovi (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 and 7 inches of snow was estimated to have fallen across Juneau County. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th as north winds gusted into the 30 to 40 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following behind the rare December severe weather outbreak, very strong winds buffeted western Wisconsin from the late evening of December 15th into the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with a few gusts over 60 mph. The highest measured wind gust was 68 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport (La Crosse County). In Gilman (Taylor County), a truck was damaged when a 10 inch pine tree fell on it. In La Crosse, several trees and power lines were blown down with some streets blocked by the downed trees. The high winds caused numerous power outages to occur across the area. On December 17th, schools were closed in Greenwood (Clark County), Sparta (Monroe County) and Thorp (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Buffalo County during the late evening of December 15th into the early morning of the 16th. State Highway 88 was closed south of Cream because of downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked northeast from the central Plains through the western Great Lakes region late Friday night (12/10) into Saturday morning (12/11), bringing 8 to 14 inches of snow to northern Wisconsin and portions of central and northeast Wisconsin. Gusty winds of 30 to 40 mph led to blowing and drifting of snow and power outages across portions of northern Wisconsin with over 14,000 customers reported without power.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 14.0 inches in Monico (Oneida Co.), 13.5 inches in Irma (Lincoln Co.), 13.0 inches near Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), 12.7 inches near Merrill (Lincoln Co.), 12.5 inches in Crivitz (southern Marinette Co.) as well as near Amberg (northern Marinette Co.), 12.0 inches in Colby (Marathon Co.) and 11.0 inches in Arbor Vitae (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total across Florence County included 13.0 inches near Spread Eagle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong low pressure area tracking to the west and north of WI, generated frequent wind gusts of 45-65 mph. Scattered tree limbs and shallow rooted trees were brought down by the winds across southern WI. Some of the trees landed on homes and power lines. Power outages were not particularly high or long in most areas.

The Winter Storm that brought over a foot of snow across portions of the southern, and eastern Twin Cities metro area, and into adjacent west-central Wisconsin, was a classic Colorado Low storm. Warnings were issued Thursday morning and had nearly 24 hours of lead time before the heaviest snowfall began across the state.||During the morning of Friday, December 10, an area of low pressure had developed across eastern Colorado. By the afternoon, and early evening, the storm moved across Kansas, and into northern Missouri. By Saturday morning, this storm system moved over the Great Lakes region. Light snow started to develop as early as Friday morning across far southern Minnesota. Regional radar noted a very elongated band of higher reflectivity over southern Minnesota that steadily moved northward Friday morning. This band was associated with strong upward mid-level forcing that remained nearly stationary across the southern part of the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin for several hours. As moisture from the main storm system moved northward into southern Minnesota Friday evening, snowfall rates intensified. Locally, snowfall rates were 1 to 2 inches per hour, with isolated higher rates. Snowfall totals by the time it tapered off, were approaching 21 inches.||Outside of this intense band of snowfall, this storm system brought widespread light snow to the area with accumulations ranging from a few inches up to a foot and a half of fresh snow. The heaviest snowfall was observed from the southwest to eastern Twin Cities metro, with amounts drastically decreasing as you moved into the northern Twin Cities.||Schools closed early due to anticipated snowfall with several businesses closing early over the south metro. The timing of the event led to numerous travel impacts, as some of the most intense snow fell during the Friday evening commute. Roadways quickly became snow covered, with reduced visibility due to intense snowfall rates.||Some of the higher snowfall totals that occurred during this event included;||18.0 inches near Hudson, Roberts, and Cameron. |14.0-16.0 inches near Baldwin, Boyceville, and Clear Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed early Friday afternoon. Several bands of light to occasional moderate snowfall occurred in the afternoon, and through the late evening. The snowfall tapered off Saturday morning. Total snowfall amounts across the county averaged between 9 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked northeast from the central Plains through the western Great Lakes region late Friday night (12/10) into Saturday morning (12/11), bringing 8 to 14 inches of snow to northern Wisconsin and portions of central and northeast Wisconsin. Gusty winds of 30 to 40 mph led to blowing and drifting of snow and power outages across portions of northern Wisconsin with over 14,000 customers reported without power.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 14.0 inches in Monico (Oneida Co.), 13.5 inches in Irma (Lincoln Co.), 13.0 inches near Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), 12.7 inches near Merrill (Lincoln Co.), 12.5 inches in Crivitz (southern Marinette Co.) as well as near Amberg (northern Marinette Co.), 12.0 inches in Colby (Marathon Co.) and 11.0 inches in Arbor Vitae (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Shawano County included 10.4 inches near Pella, 10.0 inches near Shawano, and 7.5 inches near Bonduel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of the historic severe weather outbreak of tornadoes that occurred across southern Minnesota, and into parts of western Wisconsin, a strong pressure gradient developed in response to the deepening low pressure. There were widespread wind gusts in excess of 40 mph for several hours early Thursday morning. The strongest winds, with sustained speeds of 45 mph or greater occurred from Menomonie to Eau Claire. Outside this area, wind speeds were less and averaged between 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind sensors across Chippewa County, especially near the Eau Claire airport had sustained winds of 45 mph or greater for several hours. The airport measured a wind gust of 62 mph at 1232 AM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwestern Wisconsin saw minimal improvement in drought conditions for the month of December. Only a limited area of D2 Severe Drought persisted from the Bayfield Peninsula into the Ashland area and far northern Iron County. December was a more active mouth with systems pushing through the region leading to slightly above normal precipitation. However, northern Ashland and Iron Counties maintained their normal precipitation totals for the month. While other parts of the area saw drought conditions improve to D0 Abnormally Dry, the area of D2 Severe Drought remained unchanged through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No change in the drought conditions for Bayfield County this month. Conditions ranged from D0 Abnormally Dry over the southern part of the county to D2 Severe Drought across the Bayfield Peninsula to the Chequamegon Bay area. Rainfall totals were near the climatological average with slightly higher amounts along the northern coast but not enough to impact the drought status.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of wind-driven snow lifted north and northeastward across Minnesota and into Wisconsin on the evening of Sunday, December 26th, with the snow intensifying and expanding over the northern half of the state. The snow became especially heavy late in the evening as it spread into northwest Wisconsin with snowfall rates upwards of 2 inches per hour observed. Multiple waves of heavy snow then paraded across the region eventually winding down during the afternoon of Monday, December 27th. Snow totals boasted a corridor of widespread 8 to 12 inches across northern Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches were reported across the county with the highest report of 12.5 inches recorded near Sanborn.

An area of low pressure moved across southern Wisconsin during the early morning and morning hours of November 14. This brought an area of snowfall to central and northern Wisconsin. While most of the impacts were outside of the Duluth coverage area, parts of northern Douglas County did receive around 6 inches of snow in about 12 hours as some lake enhancement occurred in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While most of the reports around Douglas County were in the 3 to 5 range, the northern part of the county did see some lake enhancement with a report of 6.3 near Poplar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving clipper system brought a narrow band of heavy snow to portions of north-central Wisconsin throughout the day on November 29. Snowfall totals of 6 to 7 inches were recorded over portions of the region.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 7.0 inches near Mountain (northern Oconto Co.), 6.8 inches in Crandon (Forest Co.), and 6.5 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total in Vilas County included 6.5 was in Eagle River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked northeast from the central Plains through the western Great Lakes region late Friday night (12/10) into Saturday morning (12/11), bringing 8 to 14 inches of snow to northern Wisconsin and portions of central and northeast Wisconsin. Gusty winds of 30 to 40 mph led to blowing and drifting of snow and power outages across portions of northern Wisconsin with over 14,000 customers reported without power.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 14.0 inches in Monico (Oneida Co.), 13.5 inches in Irma (Lincoln Co.), 13.0 inches near Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), 12.7 inches near Merrill (Lincoln Co.), 12.5 inches in Crivitz (southern Marinette Co.) as well as near Amberg (northern Marinette Co.), 12.0 inches in Colby (Marathon Co.) and 11.0 inches in Arbor Vitae (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 11.0 inches in Arbor Vitae, 10.2 inches near Boulder Junction, and 10.1 inches near Saint Germain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms tracked across central and east-central Wisconsin during the morning and early afternoon hours. The strongest storms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 63 mph was measured at the Marshfield airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwestern Wisconsin continued to fare better in terms of drought conditions for the month of November. Only a limited area of D2 Severe Drought persisted from the Bayfield Peninsula into the Ashland area and far northern Iron County. Rainfall deficits of up to one-half inch were observed across the region with slightly above normal precipitation along the Bayfield Peninsula and across parts of Ashland, Iron and Price counties. While other parts of the area saw drought conditions worsen to D1 Moderate Drought, the area of D2 Severe Drought remained unchanged through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across Bayfield County ranged from D0 Abnormally Dry over the southern part of the county to D2 Severe Drought across the Bayfield Peninsula to the Chequamegon Bay area to begin the month. Rainfall totals up to around a half inch above normal were in the northern third of the county with totals up to around a half inch below normal across the southern two thirds of the county. D1 Moderate Drought spread across the remainder of the county with no change in the area of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 2nd. No additional changes in the drought status were observed through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms across portions of east-central and southeast Wisconsin brought multiple rounds of heavy rain, resulting in flash flooding across portions of Ozaukee and Milwaukee counties late August 7th into the early morning hours on August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts quickly totaled 3 to 5 inches over southern Ozaukee County after repeated rounds of heavy rain. Street flooding was reported over this region. Reports of a foot or more of water over Green Bay Rd., just south of Falls Rd. in Grafton, and a flooded street with flowing water in Mequon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms moved across parts of southwest Wisconsin during the afternoon of August 10th. These storms produced a wind gust of 70 mph at the Platteville (Grant County) airport and a 72 mph gust was measured by the fire department in Livingston (Grant County). A funnel cloud was also sighted  in Muscoda (Grant County) as the storms rolled through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 72 mph wind gust was measured by the fire department just south of Livingston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two tornadoes touched down in Monroe County as storms moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon of August 11th. The first tornado touched down north of Norwalk (Monroe County) and moved east for six miles destroying a barn and causing tree and crop damage. The second tornado started north of Wilton (Monroe County) and moved east before dissipating southwest of Clifton (Monroe County). The damage from this tornado was limited to trees and crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell just south of Sparta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Man rescues two children from high waves and likely structural and long shore currents near North Pier, just south of North Beach on Racine Wisconsin coastline.  However he succumbed to rough and choppy waters and never emerged from the water.  About one hour later, the adult male was rescued from the water and emergency lifesaving efforts were initiated.  The male was transported to a local hospital and pronounced dead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Man succumbs to drowning in rough and choppy waters after rescuing two children.  Incident took place along North Pier, just south of North Beach along Lake Michigan in Racine.  Incoming waves were estimated to be 4 to 5 feet from nearby buoy off Milwaukee County.  Winds were north to northeast 12 to 20 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed Sunday afternoon and evening along a northward moving warm front and moisture boundary. A few individual thunderstorms developed that produced wind damage in Washington and Sheboygan counties. Otherwise most storms stayed below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of downed trees of 1 to 2 feet in diameter and tree limbs of 6 to 12 inches in diameter. Power lines were also reported to have been snapped, with lines downed at the intersection of County Road H and Highland Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few elevated thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front the morning of the 10th. One storm grew upscale into a larger complex of storms as it approached Lake Superior and produced some severe and near severe hail before moving over Lake Superior and Upper Michigan.

A complex of storms moved out of Iowa and into southern and central Wisconsin on August 10th. This complex morphed into a line of strong to severe thunderstorms, which  marched eastward and gained strength as it approached Lake Michigan. Widespread tree damage and power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ornamental tree uprooted. Small garden shed destroyed near Allentown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms moved out of Iowa and into southern and central Wisconsin on August 10th. This complex morphed into a line of strong to severe thunderstorms, which  marched eastward and gained strength as it approached Lake Michigan. Widespread tree damage and power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of a number of large trees snapped and uprooted trees across Waukesha County. Winds were estimated to be upward of 60 mph, with locally higher gusts across the southern portion of the county. Wind measurements near Eagle, WI of 68 to 77 mph were observed. A large tree fell on a house causing significant damage near Eagle, WI. A business marquee sign was knocked over in Waukesha. Hail of three-quarters of an inch was also observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was measured in Dickeyville where several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the morning hours of the 10th ahead of a northward moving warm front. One of these storms developed over a golf course and four golfers were struck by lightning just before 11:30AM. All four were injured and taken to a local hospital where they were treated for their injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four family members visiting the Hayward area from southern Wisconsin were golfing at the Big Fish Golf Club the morning of the tenth. Storms were developing in the area at that time and one of these storms produced a lightning bolt that hit near the 5th hole's tee box where the four were standing. All four were taken to an area hospital for treatment and were alert when first responders arrived. Two were released that day while the other two remained in the hospital for an unknown length of time. All four of the victims were male and their ages were 14, 19, 38, and 72.

A line of storms rolled across western Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening of September 20th ahead of a cold front. These storms produced an EF1 tornado that moved across rural areas of northern Trempealeau County. The tornado flipped a semi as it crossed State Highway 93 injuring the driver and then went on to destroy several farm outbuildings and damaged two homes south of Strum (Trempealeau County). The storms also blew down trees near Mindoro (La Crosse County), White Creek (Adams County) and Friendship (Adams County) and removed the roof off a shed west of Bloom City (Richland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down north of Independence and moved northeast for eight miles before dissipating south of Strum. Along the path of the tornado, several farm outbuildings were damaged or destroyed with a couple of houses also sustaining damage. As the tornado crossed State Highway 93 it flipped over a semi with the driver sustaining some injuries.

There was an isolated report of wind damage and several rounds of thunderstorms produced flash flooding during the overnight hours across portions of central Wisconsin as 3 to 5 inches of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dumped 3 to 5 inches of rain over Waupaca County during the evening hours. This caused several roads to be closed due to flash flooding with a report of manhole covers lifted off in Waupaca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms rolled across western Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening of September 20th ahead of a cold front. These storms produced an EF1 tornado that moved across rural areas of northern Trempealeau County. The tornado flipped a semi as it crossed State Highway 93 injuring the driver and then went on to destroy several farm outbuildings and damaged two homes south of Strum (Trempealeau County). The storms also blew down trees near Mindoro (La Crosse County), White Creek (Adams County) and Friendship (Adams County) and removed the roof off a shed west of Bloom City (Richland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was was started on fire when it was blown onto a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwestern Wisconsin continued to fare better in terms of drought conditions for the month of October. Only a limited area of D2 Severe Drought persisted from the Bayfield Peninsula into the Ashland area and far northern Iron County. Rainfall deficits of 1 to 2 inches were observed across the region and while other parts of the area saw drought conditions worsen to D0 Abnormally Dry or D1 Moderate Drought, the area of D2 Severe Drought remained unchanged through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across Bayfield County ranged from None in the far southern portion of the county to D2 Severe Drought across the Bayfield Peninsula to the Chequamegon Bay area to begin the month. Rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were observed over the month which is about an inch or so below normal. While the area of D2 Severe Drought did not change significantly through the month, the southern part of the county did worsen to D0 Abnormally Dry with the update on the 5th. No further changes were observed across the county with the subsequent updates for October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the Upper Midwest brought a round of snow to north-central Wisconsin from December 4th into the 5th. Most snowfall amounts across Taylor County were around 4 or 5 inches and this was enough to cause roads to become completely snow covered. The highest reported total was 5.5 inches east of Perkinstown (Taylor County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 5 inches of snow fell across Taylor County causing roads to become completely snow covered. The highest reported total was 5.5 inches east of Perkinstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent and strong east to northeast winds, from low pressure passing to the south,   caused scattered tree limbs to come down in the city of Racine. Also the strong and persistent easterly winds produced a higher lake level and wave action on the west end of Geneva Lake, damaging the Fontana Municipal Piers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent east to northeast winds of 25 to 30 mph and wind gusts of 40 to 47 mph brought scattered tree limbs down across the city of Racine.
