EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong thunderstorms moved east across southern WI in the vicinity of a stationary front. Some storms produced large hail.
Clusters of strong thunderstorms moved east across southern WI in the vicinity of a stationary front. Some storms produced large hail.
Dense fog contributed to a two vehicle accident with two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was a contributing factor in a deadly two vehicle accident at the intersection of highway 23 & TT just south of the Sheboygan County Memorial Airport. An eastbound mini-van struck a northbound tractor trailer. The two women in the mini-van were killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding that began in late August along the Black River near Galesville (Trempealeau County) and the Yellow River in Necedah (Juneau County) ending early in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late August on the Black River near Galesville ended very early in September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of an outflow boundary during the evening hours across east-central Wisconsin. The strongest storms produced damaging winds and a tornado. Rainfall amounts around 2 inches fell in Green Bay, producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed multiple trees in Allouez and Bellevue. The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that produced four brief tornadoes, numerous downed trees and power lines across the southern part of the Twin Cities in Minnesota, moved eastward into west-central Wisconsin and produced additional damage near Hudson and west of Roberts. ||One building that sustained significant damage was the Hudson Public Library, where the windows were blown out by debris from the roof a nearby building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped near its base, west of Roberts. Several smaller tree limbs were also blown down around the same area. This is the continuation of the strong winds that occurred in the Hudson area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pole barn had its entire roof blown off the west side, and a couple panels on the east side. The metal, some rafter parts, and a lot of cellulose loose insulation were strewn all to the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system of historic strength led to a variety of high-end weather impacts from the central plains to the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes December 15-16. An unprecedented December severe weather unfolded over portions of Minnesota and Wisconsin Wednesday evening, with the Storm Prediction Center issuing their farthest-north Moderate Risk for the Month of December. A serial derecho moving at 60-70 mph tracked from Kansas to Wisconsin, resulting in over 400 reports of damaging wind and numerous tornadoes. Most of the damage across our area occurred from south-central Minnesota through west-central Wisconsin. The strongest tornado in west central Wisconsin hit Stanley.||Before the thunderstorms, strong southerly winds led to record high temperature at many sites across the Upper Midwest. Temperatures warmed into the mid to upper 50s across much of central Minnesota and western Wisconsin, and even exceeded 60 degrees across southern Minnesota. The warm temperatures melted what was left of the snowpack from the December 10 winter storm, which led to widespread dense fog through much of the morning and afternoon.||After the thunderstorms, strong gradient winds and widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph developed for several hours late Wednesday night and into the overnight hours. The Redwood Falls airport measured a gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM.||Temperatures quickly fell below freezing across western and central Minnesota Wednesday night, causing rain to change over to a wintry mix of freezing drizzle and snow. Snowfall accumulations of 1-4 inches, along with the strong gusty winds, led to low visibility and travel hazards late Wednesday night into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mainly tree damage. Some trees fell on sheds, houses and vehicles. Two sheds had their roofs partially removed. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the central Plains and through the Upper Mississippi Valley, sweeping a cold front through the region during the late evening hours of Friday (12/15) and early morning hours of Saturday (12/16). Strong wind gusts of 55 to 76 mph were observed behind this cold front for several hours across northeast Wisconsin.  The winds led to pockets of tree damage and numerous power outages, leaving over 50,000 people without power in northeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees and power lines in northern Marinette County. Peak winds of 58 mph were measured in Pembine.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado first caused damage to groves of trees southwest of Stanley, then affected a couple farmsteads. A high tension power line was blown down. About six homes had their roofs removed, and in the northeast edge of Stanley, a roof and outer walls were removed from a portion of a house. The tornado continued into northwestern Clark County where it damaged more trees and outbuildings (see entry from NWS La Crosse).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of wind-driven snow lifted north and northeastward across Minnesota and into Wisconsin on the evening of Sunday, December 26th, with the snow intensifying and expanding over the northern half of the state. The snow became especially heavy late in the evening as it spread into northwest Wisconsin with snowfall rates upwards of 2 inches per hour observed. Multiple waves of heavy snow then paraded across the region eventually winding down during the afternoon of Monday, December 27th. Snow totals boasted a corridor of widespread 8 to 12 inches across northern Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 5 to 10 inches were reported across the county with the highest report of 13 inches coming from Lake Nebagamon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms moved across parts of western Wisconsin during the evening of June 17th. Some of these storms produced large hail across portions of Buffalo, Trempealeau, La Crosse and Vernon Counties. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter fell near Buffalo (Buffalo County) and Cochrane (Buffalo County). An estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred in Galesville (Trempealeau County) and trees were blown down that damaged roofs in Centerville (Trempealeau County).

Some of these storms produced large hail across portions of Buffalo, Trempealeau, La Crosse and Vernon Counties. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter fell near Buffalo (Buffalo County) and Cochrane (Buffalo County). An estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred in Galesville (Trempealeau County) and trees were blown down that damaged roofs in Centerville (Trempealeau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 2.5 inch diameter hail was reported in Cochrane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of relatively mild and moist air brought widespread freezing drizzle to much of southern WI. A glaze of ice accumulated on untreated roads and sidewalks. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice on untreated roads and sidewalks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system of historic strength led to a variety of high-end weather impacts from the central plains to the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes December 15-16. An unprecedented December severe weather unfolded over portions of Minnesota and Wisconsin Wednesday evening, with the Storm Prediction Center issuing their farthest-north Moderate Risk for the Month of December. A serial derecho moving at 60-70 mph tracked from Kansas to Wisconsin, resulting in over 400 reports of damaging wind and numerous tornadoes. Most of the damage across our area occurred from south-central Minnesota through west-central Wisconsin. The strongest tornado in west central Wisconsin hit Stanley.||Before the thunderstorms, strong southerly winds led to record high temperature at many sites across the Upper Midwest. Temperatures warmed into the mid to upper 50s across much of central Minnesota and western Wisconsin, and even exceeded 60 degrees across southern Minnesota. The warm temperatures melted what was left of the snowpack from the December 10 winter storm, which led to widespread dense fog through much of the morning and afternoon.||After the thunderstorms, strong gradient winds and widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph developed for several hours late Wednesday night and into the overnight hours. The Redwood Falls airport measured a gust of 78 mph at 11:30 PM.||Temperatures quickly fell below freezing across western and central Minnesota Wednesday night, causing rain to change over to a wintry mix of freezing drizzle and snow. Snowfall accumulations of 1-4 inches, along with the strong gusty winds, led to low visibility and travel hazards late Wednesday night into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pepin County primarily had minor damages ��� trees down throughout the county, a couple of houses had minor roof damage, and one farm losing an agricultural structure that was only a year old.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed across the Northern Plains and tracked east through the 6th of December bringing the first winter storm of the season to the Northland. As the low moved east it continued to intensify and slow down. While the worst conditions were further to the north, the longer residence time of the low allowed for several locations to receive heavy snowfall. The overall storm total generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches with a high report of 11 inches in Price County for the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across Douglas County were generally in the 4 to 6 inches range with 7.3 inches reported near Solon Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked from South Dakota to the northwestern Great Lakes late Sunday night (12/26) into Monday morning (12/27), bringing a period of moderate to heavy snow during the early morning, changing over to freezing drizzle later in the morning to north-central Wisconsin. Vilas County measured 6 to 8 inches of snow during the early morning before transitioning to freezing drizzle.||Some of the highest snowfall totals in Vilas County included 7.7 inches near Boulder Junction and in Land O' Lakes, 7.5 inches in Manitowish Waters, and 6.0 inches in Presque Isle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 7.7 inches near Boulder Junction and in Land O' Lakes, 7.5 inches in Manitowish Waters, and 6.0 inches in Presque Isle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy rain fell across portions of southwest Wisconsin during the early morning hours of June 26th. Radar estimates indicated that 6 to 9 inches of rain fell across the southwest in central portions of Crawford County. This caused some flash flooding to occur that sent water over roads and created mud slides. Four residences in the Town of Gays Mills sustained minor damage with access cutoff to one of these until the flood waters receded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest of Lynxville, 9.5 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed in southeastern Colorado and tracked northeastward across the Midwest December 10th into the 11th. Good model agreement prior to the onset of snow led to a high confidence of heavy snowfall. A swath of heavy snow stretched from southern Minnesota into portions of northwest Wisconsin. Multiple waves of snowfall resulted in 6 to 12 inches of snow across parts of northwest Wisconsin with parts of Price County seeing over 15 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ashland County ended up on the northern edge of the system with most of the county receiving less that 6 inches of snow. However, the far southeastern corner did receive 6 to 7 inches of accumulation with a high report of 7.5 inches in Butternut.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from North Dakota through the northwestern Great Lakes region during the day on Sunday (12/5), bringing an occluded front through the area during the evening hours before tracking east Monday morning (12/6). A band of moderate to heavy snow developed Sunday morning, ending around midday across most of the region, with continued light snow and blowing snow across north-central Wisconsin through the early evening hours.  A band of 6 to 10 inches fell across north-central and northeast Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 10.0 inches near Rhinelander and in Woodboro (Oneida Co.), as well as Heafford Junction (Lincoln Co.), and Marinette (Marinette Co.), 9.8 inches in Presque Isle (Vilas Co.), 9.4 inches near Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.), 9.0 inches near Peshtigo (southern Marinette Co.), near Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), and near Athelstane (northern Marinette Co.), 8.3 inches near Argonne (Forest Co.), and 8.0 inches near Ellison Bay (Door Co.) as well as in Oconto Falls (southern Oconto Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 9.8 inches in Presque Isle and 6.9 inches near Boulder Junction as well as near Saint Germain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system slid across the Northland on the 21st bringing widespread snowfall across the region. While this system carried some Pacific moisture with it, overall moisture was pretty limited. However, favorable dynamic conditions with the system allowed for higher snow ratios and higher snowfall than expected. While most areas remained under 6 inches of accumulation with this system, a few higher reports of 6 to 8 inches were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 inches in six hours were recorded near Wascott during the morning of the 21st.

A band of moderate to heavy snow developed Sunday morning, ending around midday across most of the region, with continued light snow and blowing snow across north-central Wisconsin through the early evening hours.  A band of 6 to 10 inches fell across north-central and northeast Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 10.0 inches near Rhinelander and in Woodboro (Oneida Co.), as well as Heafford Junction (Lincoln Co.), and Marinette (Marinette Co.), 9.8 inches in Presque Isle (Vilas Co.), 9.4 inches near Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.), 9.0 inches near Peshtigo (southern Marinette Co.), near Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), and near Athelstane (northern Marinette Co.), 8.3 inches near Argonne (Forest Co.), and 8.0 inches near Ellison Bay (Door Co.) as well as in Oconto Falls (southern Oconto Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across southern Marinette County included 10.0 inches in Marinette, 9.0 inches near Peshtigo, and 6.5 inches near Caldron Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed during the morning hours across northeast Wisconsin and continued through the late afternoon hours on Friday (12/15).  The fog contributed to a vehicle crash, which pushed a car onto train tracks and led to a train derailment on highway 141 just north of Pound around 11:17 am CST. One person died and a couple of people were injured. The accident caused $10,000 worth of damage to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog contributed to a vehicle crash on highway 141 just north of Pound around 11:17 am CST.  The vehicle crash pushed a car onto train tracks, leading to a train derailment on highway 141.  One person died and a couple of people were injured. The accident caused $10,000 worth of damage to public infrastructure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over eastern Colorado early on the 28th and moved northeastward through the day across the Central Plains and into Upper Michigan by sunrise on the 29th. While most of the heavier snow with this system fell across northeastern Minnesota, parts of the Bayfield Peninsula did receive around 6 inches of snowfall by the time the storm departed the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of up to 5 inches were received from around the county, but some terrain-influenced heavier snowfall was observed along the Bayfield Peninsula to the northeast of the city of Bayfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong area of low pressure moved across the Nation's midsection on the 15th into the 16th and brought severe storms, including a tornado outbreak, on the warm side of the system with snow and strong winds on the cold side of the system. The northern end of the line of storms along the cold front moved across northwest Wisconsin during the evening of the 15th with wind gusts to 40-50 mph in spots as the storms weekend. Winds then gusted to 40 mph through the overnight hours and led to numerous downed trees and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 40 mph across the county during the night of the 15th into the 16th resulted in widespread reports of trees snapped off and uprooted across the county leading to tree fires and power outages affecting over 500 customers along with blocked roads. At some point during the event, every fire department, ambulance service and law enforcement agency in the county responded to a call in the blowing snow and winds. No structures were damaged during the event. It is unknown if the tree damage occurred during the late evening thunderstorms or the gusty winds overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historical event unfolded across the central United States on Friday (12/15) as a strong storm system tracked across the central Plains and through the Upper Mississippi Valley. Record warmth was observed across the state of Wisconsin ahead of this system, as temperatures soared into the 60s. ||A weakening line of severe thunderstorms tracked across central Wisconsin during the late evening hours, producing damaging winds before weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted several trees, lifted a shed from its foundation, and caused damage to a garage and house in Colby.  The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the central Plains and through the Upper Mississippi Valley, sweeping a cold front through the region during the late evening hours of Friday (12/15) and early morning hours of Saturday (12/16). Strong wind gusts of 55 to 76 mph were observed behind this cold front for several hours across northeast Wisconsin.  The winds led to pockets of tree damage and numerous power outages, leaving over 50,000 people without power in northeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees and power lines in Oneida County. Peak winds of 76 mph were measured at the Rhinelander airport.

The winds led to pockets of tree damage and numerous power outages, leaving over 50,000 people without power in northeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees and power lines in Langlade County. Peak winds of 53 mph were measured at the Antigo airport with sustained winds over 40 mph for over an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in less than a week, storms producing locally heavy rains moved across Crawford County in southwest Wisconsin. The runoff from the rain caused standing high water, mud and rock slides that closed a portion of State Highway 35 west of Eastman. Radar estimates indicated up to 3 inches of rain fell on ground that was already saturated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from storms with locally heavy rain caused standing high water, mud and rock slides that caused officials to close State Highway 35 between County Highways D and N west of Eastman. Additional county and township roads and bridges were damaged by floodwaters near Seneca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow moved across western Wisconsin during the late morning and afternoon of December 28th. Roads quickly became snow covered and slippery and contributed to a fatal accident that occurred on Interstate 90 near West Salem (La Crosse County). A semi was slowing down because of backed up traffic and a car behind the semi was not able to stop, hit the back of the trailer and became trapped underneath. The driver of the car died as a result of the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The driver of a car was killed in a two vehicle accident on Interstate 90 near West Salem. Roads were snow covered and slippery from snow that fell from late morning through the afternoon. A semi was slowing down for backup traffic and a car behind the semi was unable to stop in the slippery conditions, slid under the trailer and became trapped. The driver of the car was killed as a result of the sustained injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Upper Midwest from December 10th into the 11th. The storm brought snow and strong winds to most of western Wisconsin. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 11 inches. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred as gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th. The highest reported totals were 11.5 inches near Medford (Taylor County) and 10 inches near Owen (Clark County) and in Mondovi (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 2 to 5 inches of snow fell across Vernon County. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th when north winds gusted into the 30 to 40 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall varied widely across northwestern Wisconsin during the month of September with  the region remaining in D1 Moderate Drought or lower. Some areas saw slightly above normal rainfall for the month, but with year-to-date deficits still in excess of 3 to 7 inches, drought conditions persisted. An area of D2 Severe Drought was introduced during the first week of the month for areas near Lake Superior. A wet period in the middle of the month led to a reduction in area of D2 Severe Drought and becoming more focused from the Bayfield Peninsula eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: September began with most of the county being in D1 Moderate Drought with the southeastern corner being in D0 Abnormally Dry. Less than a quarter of an inch of rainfall fell over the first week of the month and the continued dry conditions led to an introduction of an area of D2 Significant Drought across the northern third of the county on September 7th. Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches during the middle part of the month helped to improve the drought conditions and the area of D2 Severe Drought was removed with the update on the 21st. The month ended up being around an inch below normal for rainfall overall, but no additional changes in the drought status were seen.

Less than a quarter of an inch of rainfall fell over the first week or so of the month and the continued dry conditions led to an introduction of an area of D2 Significant Drought across the northern third of the county on September 7th. Rainfall of 1 to 3 inches during the middle part of the month helped to improve the drought conditions and the area of D2 Severe Drought was reduced in size with the update on the 21st becoming more focused across the Bayfield Peninsula and the Apostle Islands. The month ended up being around a half inch below normal for rainfall overall, but no additional changes in the drought status were seen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving east across the Upper Midwest propelled a cold front through northwest Wisconsin leading to the development of a line of storms during the afternoon. These storms produced sporadic wind damage and heavy rain and training of the storms led to some minor flash flooding in spots as well. While storms were exhibiting circulation in the low levels, no tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was occurring on US Highway 63 in front of a gas station with the water up to the grills on cars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic tree and power line damage at this location and near Lake Nabagamon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The local sheriff in Dunn County reported two individuals that were working at a construction site in Menomonie, Wisconsin, Tuesday morning, August 24th, were struck by lightning.||A 60-year-old man was pronounced dead at the scene. A 20-year-old man was taken to Mayo in Eau Claire with serious injuries.||Dunn County authorities said the crew was working on a pole shed under a partial roof on ladders when the lightning struck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck two construction workers. One died and the other was transported to a local hospital with serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early Sunday morning, August 29th, a broken line of storms that formed across eastern Minnesota, moved across west central Wisconsin. There were several stronger cores that developed and one produced a severe wind gust at the Eau Claire, Wisconsin airport.

An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across western Wisconsin. Eight tornadoes occurred across parts of Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. Four of these tornadoes were rated EF2 and three EF1. The most substantial damage from the EF2 tornadoes occurred in Clark County. One tornado destroyed a home in the southwest corner of the county near Five Mile Creek west of Neillsville (Clark County). Another tornado caused extensive damage to farms and trees after moving through Stanley (Chippewa County) into the northwest corner of Clark County. An EF0 tornado passed through Perrot State Park (Trempealeau County) and damaged several homes on the west edge of Trempealeau (Trempealeau County). The other tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings. Because of the severe weather threat, the Rotary Lights display in La Crosse (La Crosse County) was closed for the first time in over 26 years where two temporary structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph wind gust was measured near Liberty Pole with a private weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and early evening of Saturday, August, 7th, a line of thunderstorms developed across west central Wisconsin. These storms remained nearly stationary for an hour or two and produced over 5 inches of rainfall in a short period of time across Eau Claire. There were social media pictures of standing water over several feet in a few neighborhoods. There were several homes that had water in their basement due to the flood waters. In addition, 1 to 2 feet of water was observed at the intersection of Eddy and Abbie Hill drive where a few cars stalled out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a measured rainfall total of 6.52 inches in the city of Eau Claire that most fell in a 2 hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was another observed rainfall report of 5.26 inches that fell in the city of Eau Claire. Most of that rainfall total happened in a 2 hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 1 to 2 feet of water at the intersection of Eddy Lane and Abbie Hill Drive. A few cars stalled out in this intersection. In addition, there were a few residential areas that had flooded basements due to the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following behind the rare December severe weather outbreak, very strong winds buffeted western Wisconsin from the late evening of December 15th into the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with a few gusts over 60 mph. The highest measured wind gust was 68 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport (La Crosse County). In Gilman (Taylor County), a truck was damaged when a 10 inch pine tree fell on it. In La Crosse, several trees and power lines were blown down with some streets blocked by the downed trees. The high winds caused numerous power outages to occur across the area. On December 17th, schools were closed in Greenwood (Clark County), Sparta (Monroe County) and Thorp (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Clark County during the early morning of December 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of freezing drizzle moved across portions of western Wisconsin during the early morning of December 23rd. A glaze of ice accumulated on untreated surfaces, including Interstate 94 in Jackson County. A  38 vehicle accident occurred on the icy roads near Northfield (Jackson County). Some of the vehicles were destroyed in a fire but no deaths occurred with about 20 minor injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions developed on Interstate 94 after a band of freezing drizzle moved across the area. A 38 vehicle accident occurred near Northfield and some vehicles were destroyed in a fire. No deaths occurred with about 20 minor injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across western Wisconsin. Eight tornadoes occurred across parts of Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. Four of these tornadoes were rated EF2 and three EF1. The most substantial damage from the EF2 tornadoes occurred in Clark County. One tornado destroyed a home in the southwest corner of the county near Five Mile Creek west of Neillsville (Clark County). Another tornado caused extensive damage to farms and trees after moving through Stanley (Chippewa County) into the northwest corner of Clark County. An EF0 tornado passed through Perrot State Park (Trempealeau County) and damaged several homes on the west edge of Trempealeau (Trempealeau County). The other tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings. Because of the severe weather threat, the Rotary Lights display in La Crosse (La Crosse County) was closed for the first time in over 26 years where two temporary structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows were blown out of buildings and roofs were removed from sheds in Thorp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following behind the rare December severe weather outbreak, very strong winds buffeted western Wisconsin from the late evening of December 15th into the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with a few gusts over 60 mph. The highest measured wind gust was 68 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport (La Crosse County). In Gilman (Taylor County), a truck was damaged when a 10 inch pine tree fell on it. In La Crosse, several trees and power lines were blown down with some streets blocked by the downed trees. The high winds caused numerous power outages to occur across the area. On December 17th, schools were closed in Greenwood (Clark County), Sparta (Monroe County) and Thorp (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Richland County during the late evening of December 15th into the early morning of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Upper Midwest from December 10th into the 11th. The storm brought snow and strong winds to most of western Wisconsin. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 11 inches. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred as gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th. The highest reported totals were 11.5 inches near Medford (Taylor County) and 10 inches near Owen (Clark County) and in Mondovi (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across Monroe County. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th when north winds gusted into the 30 to 40 mph range. The highest reported snowfall total was 7.3 inches north of Four Corners and in Cataract.
